item.description:
Return to Office as a Response to “Simulation” Instability

Remote work surged during the COVID-19 pandemic, but many companies have since pushed Return-to-Office (RTO) mandates. While productivity and culture are the oft-cited reasons, an emerging theoretical view is that RTO responds to a deeper crisis of simulation – a breakdown in trust and reality verification in virtual workflows. Recent incidents with deepfakes, identity fraud, AI impersonation, and “unverifiable” remote workers have challenged organizations’ ability to know what (and who) is real online. This report explores real-world cases from 2020 onward where companies enforced RTO or similar measures following such incidents, and draws on theory (Baudrillard, Giddens, Deleuze, Foucault, Mbembe, Massumi, systems theory) to frame RTO as an attempt to restore stability in the face of simulation collapse. We also survey current discourse on synthetic labor, workplace deepfakes, epistemic instability in remote systems, and the potential collapse of trust in digital work environments.

It’s not that COVID isn’t still here. It’s that most of us have trained ourselves to behave like it isn’t, and built our worlds around the enforcement of that behavior.

We’re in a moment where the virological risk—of repeat infection, of permanent immune disruption, of cumulative neurological damage—is still climbing. Transmission is constant. The virus mutates fast, stacks risk fast. There is no herd immunity horizon. No safety-in-numbers. Long COVID is still under-researched, still disabling people with one infection, and more reliably with each reinfection. The scientific consensus is not subtle: SARS-CoV-2 is airborne, cumulative, and actively destructive to our capacity to live, relate, and organize. This is happening.

item.title: The Illusion of Clarity in a Confused World
item.description:
Hey.
You.
Yeah—you, reading this in a browser tab behind an Excel sheet or a Figma prototype or a Jira ticket.
Scrolling to figure out what the hell is going on in the world.
Curious. Concerned. Maybe even a little hopeful that with enough information, something might finally make sense.

With a healthy awareness of the irony, I’m going to try and explain something to you.
This place—this internet, this job, this mind you’ve been given to think with—it’s built to keep you confused.
That confusion? That’s not a glitch in the system.
That is the system.
